_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Iberia Parish, Louisiana. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for the lower Bayou Teche parish anchored by New Iberia. Iberia Parish sits at the junction of Cajun prairie, sugar country, and Gulf marsh -- home to Avery Island (Tabasco, salt mining), significant sugar plantations, and one of Acadiana's most culturally diverse populations including Acadians, Creoles, Spanish Islenos, and gens de couleur libres.

current_courthouse.location: New Iberia, LA -- 300 Iberia Street. Art Deco courthouse in the center of New Iberia, facing the Bayou Teche.

previous_courthouses.location: New Iberia -- frame courthouse near Bayou Teche. Iberia Parish was created in 1868 from St. Martin and St. Mary parishes.
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by a more substantial structure as the parish grew.

previous_courthouses.location: New Iberia -- brick courthouse on Main Street facing Bayou Teche
previous_courthouses.fate: Served the parish through the sugar boom and early oil era. Replaced by the current WPA-era courthouse. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.cause: Parish creation -- Iberia was carved from St. Martin and St. Mary parishes in 1868
records_survived: Pre-1868 records remain at St. Martin (St. Martinville) and St. Mary (Franklin) parish courthouses
record_losses.description: Iberia Parish was created in 1868 during Reconstruction from portions of St. Martin Parish (north and west of Bayou Teche) and St. Mary Parish (south and east). All records predating 1868 for the New Iberia area are split between the St. Martin Parish Clerk of Court at St. Martinville and the St. Mary Parish Clerk of Court at Franklin. Colonial-era records for the Attakapas district are primarily at St. Martinville. This split-parish origin creates a research challenge -- the researcher must determine which side of the old boundary a property fell on.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in the New Iberia area before 1868, their records are split between two courthouses -- St. Martinville and Franklin -- depending on which side of Bayou Teche the family's land lay.
record_losses.source: Brasseaux, Carl A. The Founding of New Acadia. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1987.

record_losses.cause: Last Island Hurricane -- catastrophic storm that devastated the Gulf coast
records_destroyed: Some early records at the St. Mary Parish side may have been affected; Iberia-area impact was primarily economic
records_survived: Courthouse records were inland at St. Martinville and Franklin and survived
record_losses.description: The Last Island Hurricane of 1856 destroyed the resort at Isle Derniere and devastated the lower Bayou Teche and Atchafalaya region. While the courthouse records at St. Martinville and Franklin survived (they were inland), the storm destroyed plantation records, personal papers, and business documents along the lower Teche and Vermilion Bay. Sugar planters lost not only their crops and buildings but often their private record collections.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The 1856 hurricane's destruction of plantation records means that some private family papers, slave records, and business documents for the lower Teche area were lost even though the courthouse records survived.
record_losses.source: Sothern, Elmer. Isle Derniere: The End of Last Island. Houma, LA: self-published, 1998.

records_survived: All record series from 1868 forward appear intact
record_losses.description: Iberia Parish has not experienced a catastrophic courthouse fire or flood since its creation. The primary challenges for researchers are the split-parish origin (requiring research at two courthouses for pre-1868 records) and the cultural-linguistic complexity of records in French, Spanish, and English reflecting the diverse population.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Iberia Parish's post-1868 records are intact, but they document one of the most culturally complex populations in Louisiana -- Acadians, Creoles, gens de couleur libres, Spanish Islenos, Anglo-Americans, and formerly enslaved people all appear in the same record books.
record_losses.source: Kane, Harnett T. The Bayous of Louisiana. New York: William Morrow, 1943.

deeds.completeness: Good -- continuous conveyance records from parish creation. Pre-1868 conveyances split between St. Martin and St. Mary parishes.
deeds.gaps: No gaps post-1868. Colonial-era land grants for the Attakapas district are at St. Martinville and the Louisiana State Archives.
deeds.repository: Iberia Parish Clerk of Court, New Iberia
deeds.online_access: Limited -- some indexes through Louisiana Secretary of State

wills_estates.completeness: Good -- succession records from parish creation. Antebellum succession inventories for the New Iberia area are at St. Martin and St. Mary parishes and are exceptionally detailed for the sugar plantation era.
wills_estates.gaps: Pre-1868 successions at St. Martin (St. Martinville) and St. Mary (Franklin). Sugar plantation inventories with enslaved person names are among the most genealogically valuable documents in Louisiana.
wills_estates.repository: Iberia Parish Clerk of Court, New Iberia

marriages.completeness: Good from parish creation. Catholic church records at St. Peter's in New Iberia and neighboring churches supplement civil records from the 1840s.
marriages.gaps: Pre-1868 civil marriages at St. Martin and St. Mary parishes. Church records significantly predate civil records.
marriages.repository: Iberia Parish Clerk of Court; Diocese of Lafayette archives
marriages.online_access: Some indexes at Louisiana Secretary of State

court_minutes.gaps: Pre-1868 court proceedings at St. Martin and St. Mary parishes
court_minutes.repository: Iberia Parish Clerk of Court, New Iberia

vital_records.completeness: Fair -- compliance improved over time. Church records are more reliable for births before the 1920s.
vital_records.repository: Louisiana Office of Vital Records, New Orleans

substitute_sources.source_type: St. Martin and St. Mary Parish records (pre-1868)
substitute_sources.description: Records for the New Iberia area before 1868 are split between St. Martin Parish at St. Martinville (for the area north and west of Bayou Teche) and St. Mary Parish at Franklin (for the area south and east). Both courthouses hold colonial-era records essential for tracing Acadian and Creole families.
substitute_sources.repository: St. Martin Parish Clerk of Court, St. Martinville; St. Mary Parish Clerk of Court, Franklin
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for all Iberia Parish families before 1868. The St. Martin records include the earliest Attakapas colonial records. The St. Mary records document the lower Teche sugar plantations. The researcher must determine the correct parent parish based on the property's location relative to Bayou Teche.
substitute_sources.source: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.

substitute_sources.description: St. Peter's Church in New Iberia, Our Lady of the Lake at Loreauville, and churches in Jeanerette, Delcambre, and surrounding communities maintain sacramental registers predating civil records. Records are in French and document the multi-ethnic population.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual parish churches; Diocese of Lafayette archives
substitute_sources.era_coverage: 1840s-present (New Iberia); 1760s at St. Martin de Tours, St. Martinville
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Catholic records document the cultural complexity of the New Iberia area -- Acadian, Creole, Spanish, and Anglo families all recorded in the same parish registers. Godparent selection reveals cross-cultural alliances.

substitute_sources.source_type: Avery Island and salt mine records
substitute_sources.description: The Avery Island salt mine (operated by the Avery and McIlhenny families) and the Tabasco sauce operation generated employment records, payroll books, and company archives from the 1860s forward. These records document workers -- including formerly enslaved people and their descendants -- who lived and worked on the island.
substitute_sources.repository: McIlhenny Company Archives (private, limited access); LSU Special Collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: For families connected to Avery Island, company records may supplement courthouse records with employment dates, wages, and family details not found elsewhere.
substitute_sources.source: Bernard, Shane K. Tabasco: An Illustrated History. Avery Island, LA: McIlhenny Company, 2007.

narrative_hooks: Iberia Parish did not exist before 1868 -- {ancestor_name}'s earlier records are split between St. Martinville and Franklin, two courthouses in two different parishes, depending on which side of Bayou Teche the family's land lay.

The succession inventories at St. Martinville and Franklin list every enslaved person by name on the lower Teche sugar plantations -- if {ancestor_name} was enslaved in the New Iberia area, these inventories are likely the richest surviving source.

If {ancestor_name} was part of the gens de couleur libres community in New Iberia, the notarial archives may document a family that owned property, made contracts, and navigated a three-tier racial system that existed nowhere else in North America.

The salt from Avery Island was old before the Acadians arrived -- if {ancestor_name} worked the salt mine or the Tabasco fields, company records on that island may hold family details the courthouse does not.
source_references: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. 39 vols. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.
Brasseaux, Carl A. The Founding of New Acadia. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1987.
Bernard, Shane K. Tabasco: An Illustrated History. Avery Island, LA: McIlhenny Company, 2007.
Kane, Harnett T. The Bayous of Louisiana. New York: William Morrow, 1943.
Winters, John D. The Civil War in Louisiana. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1963.
